facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Louis XIV officially move the French royal court to the Palace of Versailles?
facts.explanation: Construction at Versailles began in 1661 under Louis XIV, but the king formally moved the royal court and government to the palace in 1682. The move consolidated power by bringing the entire French nobility under the king's watchful eye, reducing their ability to conspire.
facts.statement: Louis XIV moved the French royal court to Versailles in 1682.
facts.wowFactor: Versailles has 2,143 windows, 1,252 chimneys, and 700 rooms — and receives about 15 million visitors per year, making it one of the most visited palaces on Earth.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a grand golden palace facade reflected in a vast rectangular fountain under a clear blue sky

facts.quizQuestion: The Treaty of Versailles, which formally ended World War I, was signed in what year at the Palace of Versailles?
facts.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1919, in the Hall of Mirrors at the Palace of Versailles. The location was chosen deliberately — it was the same hall where the German Empire had been proclaimed in 1871 after the Franco-Prussian War, making the signing a pointed act of symbolic reversal.
facts.statement: The Treaty of Versailles ending World War I was signed at the Palace of Versailles in 1919.
facts.wowFactor: The Hall of Mirrors where the treaty was signed has 357 mirrors and 20,000 candles. Germany's defeat was signed in the same room where Germany had humiliated France 48 years earlier.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an ornate gilded hall lined with enormous mirrors reflecting candlelight, diplomats gathered at a long table

facts.quizQuestion: Roughly how many buildings are contained within Beijing's Forbidden City complex?
facts.explanation: The Forbidden City contains approximately 980 surviving buildings spread across 72 hectares, with an estimated 8,886 rooms. It served as the imperial palace for 24 emperors across the Ming and Qing dynasties over more than 500 years.
facts.statement: The Forbidden City contains approximately 980 buildings across 72 hectares.
facts.wowFactor: Legend says the Forbidden City has 9,999.5 rooms — just short of the 10,000 rooms said to exist in the Heavenly Palace — so the emperor could remain humble before heaven. The actual count is closer to 8,886.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a sweeping aerial view of red-walled imperial buildings with golden curved rooftops stretching to the horizon

facts.quizQuestion: Which master builder is credited as the principal designer and construction supervisor of the Forbidden City, built between 1406 and 1420?
facts.explanation: Kuai Xiang served as the principal designer of the Forbidden City, which was constructed between 1406 and 1420 during the reign of the Yongle Emperor. The project employed roughly a million workers and brought in timber from Sichuan and stone from quarries near Beijing.
facts.statement: Kuai Xiang was the principal designer of the Forbidden City, built 1406-1420.
facts.wowFactor: The Forbidden City is now the Palace Museum and receives approximately 17 million visitors per year — more than the Louvre and the British Museum combined.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an ancient Chinese architect unrolling a scroll blueprint before a vast red-and-gold palace under construction

facts.quizQuestion: According to Guinness World Records, Neuschwanstein Castle in Bavaria is the world's tallest castle. Approximately how tall is it?
facts.explanation: Neuschwanstein Castle stands 65 metres (213 feet) tall and is recognized by Guinness World Records as the world's tallest castle. It was built starting in 1869 for King Ludwig II of Bavaria, who envisioned it as a personal retreat and tribute to the operas of Richard Wagner.
facts.statement: Neuschwanstein Castle is 65 metres tall, making it the world's tallest castle per Guinness World Records.
facts.wowFactor: Ludwig II lived in Neuschwanstein for only 172 days before his mysterious death in 1886 — the castle was never even finished. Disney's Sleeping Beauty Castle was directly inspired by it.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a white fairy-tale castle with pointed towers rising dramatically above pine forests and a misty mountain valley

facts.quizQuestion: Which famous Disney theme park castle was directly inspired by Neuschwanstein Castle in Bavaria?
facts.explanation: Walt Disney was inspired by Neuschwanstein Castle when designing Sleeping Beauty Castle at Disneyland in Anaheim, California, which opened in 1955. The fairy-tale silhouette of Neuschwanstein — with its steep towers and dramatic mountain setting — became the template for Disney's iconic castle imagery.
facts.statement: Disney's Sleeping Beauty Castle was inspired by Neuschwanstein Castle in Bavaria.
facts.wowFactor: Neuschwanstein has received over 61 million total visitors. It was added to the UNESCO World Heritage tentative list in 2025, over 130 years after construction began.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art split-panel showing Neuschwanstein's white turrets on the left and a colourful cartoon version of a Disney castle on the right

facts.quizQuestion: Which Islamic dynasty built the Alhambra palace complex in Granada, Spain, beginning in 1238?
facts.explanation: The Alhambra was built beginning in 1238 by the Nasrid dynasty, the last Muslim rulers of the Iberian Peninsula. It is considered the best-preserved medieval Islamic palace in the world, renowned for its intricate geometric tilework and carved stucco decorations.
facts.statement: The Alhambra was built by the Nasrid dynasty starting in 1238 and is the best-preserved medieval Islamic palace.
facts.wowFactor: In 1492, Ferdinand and Isabella received Christopher Columbus at the Alhambra and gave him royal endorsement for his voyage. The same year they conquered the palace from the last Nasrid sultan.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an arched Islamic courtyard with intricate geometric tile patterns, a central fountain, and slender columns

facts.quizQuestion: In 1492, where did Christopher Columbus receive royal endorsement from Ferdinand and Isabella for his voyage to the Americas?
facts.explanation: Columbus received his royal endorsement at the Alhambra palace in Granada in January 1492 — the same year Ferdinand and Isabella completed the Reconquista by conquering the last Nasrid sultanate. The convergence of these events at one place makes the Alhambra a pivotal site in world history.
facts.statement: Columbus received royal endorsement for his 1492 voyage at the Alhambra in Granada.
facts.wowFactor: Washington Irving wrote a celebrated book, 'Tales of the Alhambra' (1832), after living in the palace's empty apartments. His romantic accounts helped spark an international fascination that saved the crumbling palace from further neglect.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Columbus presenting plans to a king and queen in a Moorish palace with horseshoe arches and starry tilework

facts.quizQuestion: Who founded the Tower of London in 1066 and began construction of the White Tower around 1078?
facts.explanation: William the Conqueror established the Tower of London after his victory at the Battle of Hastings in 1066. He built the White Tower around 1078 as a symbol of Norman power. Over the centuries the complex grew to cover 12 acres and served as a royal residence, armory, treasury, mint, and prison.
facts.statement: William the Conqueror founded the Tower of London in 1066, building the White Tower around 1078.
facts.wowFactor: Legend says the Tower of London will fall — and with it the Crown and the kingdom — if its resident ravens ever leave. The birds have their wings clipped just in case. The Ravenmaster is a real official position.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a stern Norman knight before a grey stone fortress tower with a Union Jack banner, ravens perched on the battlements

facts.quizQuestion: At approximately what altitude above sea level does the Potala Palace sit in Lhasa, Tibet?
facts.explanation: The Potala Palace sits at an altitude of approximately 3,700 metres (12,100 feet) above sea level in Lhasa, Tibet. The palace itself rises 119 metres above Marpo Ri hill and more than 300 metres above the valley floor. It served as the Dalai Lama's winter palace until 1959 and is now a museum.
facts.statement: The Potala Palace sits at approximately 3,700 metres altitude in Lhasa, Tibet.
facts.wowFactor: The modern Potala Palace was built by the 5th Dalai Lama starting in 1649 and contains over 1,000 rooms. Its walls are up to 5 metres thick at the base — partly for earthquake resistance, partly because construction used a mortar mixed with milk, honey, and animal blood.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a vast white-and-red palace rising tier by tier up a rocky hill against a vivid Himalayan sky

facts.quizQuestion: How many rooms does Buckingham Palace have?
facts.explanation: Buckingham Palace has 775 rooms in total, including 19 State rooms, 52 royal and guest bedrooms, 188 staff bedrooms, 92 offices, and 78 bathrooms. The core structure dates to 1703, but it became the official royal residence in 1837 under Queen Victoria.
facts.statement: Buckingham Palace has 775 rooms and became the official royal residence in 1837.
facts.wowFactor: During World War II a German bomb destroyed the palace chapel. Rather than leave the site empty, it was converted into the Queen's Gallery — now a public art gallery that opens several months per year. The Royal Standard flies when the monarch is home; the Union Flag flies when they are away.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a grand cream-coloured palace facade with the Royal Standard flying above the gates as a crowd gathers below

facts.quizQuestion: Windsor Castle, the oldest and largest inhabited castle in the world, was originally founded by which Norman king in the 11th century?
facts.explanation: Windsor Castle was founded by William the Conqueror in the 11th century as a royal fortress. Spanning 13 acres, it is the oldest and largest occupied castle in the world and has been home to more than 40 British monarchs across over 900 years of continuous royal use.
facts.statement: Windsor Castle was founded by William the Conqueror and is the oldest and largest inhabited castle, home to over 40 monarchs.
facts.wowFactor: St George's Chapel at Windsor Castle is one of the finest examples of Gothic architecture in England and serves as the burial place for ten British monarchs, including Henry VIII and his third wife Jane Seymour.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive grey stone castle with round towers and a flag on the keep, set on a broad green hill

facts.quizQuestion: The Spoonmaker's Diamond on display at Topkapi Palace in Istanbul weighs how many carats?
facts.explanation: The Spoonmaker's Diamond (Kaşıkçı Elması) at Topkapi Palace weighs 86 carats and is one of the largest diamonds on public display in the world. Topkapi Palace was built by Mehmed the Conqueror starting in 1459 and served as the administrative centre of the Ottoman Empire for nearly 400 years.
facts.statement: The Spoonmaker's Diamond at Topkapi Palace weighs 86 carats and is one of the world's largest diamonds on public display.
facts.wowFactor: According to legend, the diamond was found by a poor fisherman who traded it to a spoon-maker for three wooden spoons — not knowing its value. The name 'Spoonmaker's Diamond' stuck even after the stone reached the Ottoman treasury.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive teardrop diamond glittering in an ornate display case inside a domed Ottoman palace treasury

facts.quizQuestion: Which Ottoman sultan ordered the construction of Topkapi Palace in Istanbul, beginning in 1459?
facts.explanation: Topkapi Palace was built on the orders of Mehmed the Conqueror (Mehmed II) beginning in 1459, six years after his conquest of Constantinople. The palace covered between 59,260 and 70,000 square metres and served as the seat of Ottoman power for nearly 400 years, until Sultan Abdulmecid I moved to Dolmabahce Palace in 1853.
facts.statement: Mehmed the Conqueror built Topkapi Palace starting in 1459, after conquering Constantinople.
facts.wowFactor: Topkapi Palace became a museum in 1924, just one year after the founding of the Turkish Republic. Today it displays one of the world's finest collections of Ottoman art, Chinese porcelain, and Islamic relics.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a stern Ottoman sultan on horseback surveying a sprawling palace complex overlooking a sparkling blue strait

facts.quizQuestion: What is the popular nickname of Himeji Castle in Japan, given for its gleaming white plastered exterior?
facts.explanation: Himeji Castle is nicknamed 'White Heron Castle' (Shirasagi-jo or Hakuro-jo) because its brilliant white plaster exterior and graceful silhouette resemble a white heron in flight. The main keep, completed in 1609, stands 46.4 metres tall and is part of a complex of 83 buildings.
facts.statement: Himeji Castle in Japan is nicknamed 'White Heron Castle' for its brilliant white exterior.
facts.wowFactor: Himeji Castle famously survived World War II despite the surrounding city of Himeji being heavily bombed — the castle was never struck. It also has a hidden 'dummy floor' visible from outside but inaccessible from inside, designed to confuse enemy attackers.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a gleaming white multi-tiered Japanese castle with sweeping curved rooftops rising above pink cherry blossoms

facts.quizQuestion: Construction of Himeji Castle's famous main keep began in what year?
facts.explanation: Although a fortress first stood on the site from 1333, the main keep of Himeji Castle was constructed between 1601 and 1609 under the direction of Ikeda Terumasa. The castle was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1993 and is one of only twelve original castles surviving in Japan.
facts.statement: Himeji Castle's main keep was built between 1601 and 1609 by Ikeda Terumasa.
facts.wowFactor: The original castle on this site dates to 1333, but the spectacular main keep that stands today was built in just eight years. Its complex defensive maze of paths, gates, and dead-ends was designed so invaders would become lost before reaching the inner citadel.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a white tiered Japanese castle keep seen from a narrow stone path flanked by thick walls, under an autumn sky

facts.quizQuestion: How many windows does the Palace of Versailles have?
facts.explanation: The Palace of Versailles has 2,143 windows, along with 700 rooms and 1,252 chimneys. The palace was originally a hunting lodge for Louis XIII before Louis XIV transformed it into Europe's greatest royal palace beginning in 1661, using architects Louis Le Vau and Jules Hardouin-Mansart.
facts.statement: The Palace of Versailles has 2,143 windows, 700 rooms, and 1,252 chimneys.
facts.wowFactor: Versailles attracts approximately 15 million visitors per year. On a single peak summer day, the gardens can hold over 50,000 visitors — more than the entire population of the town of Versailles itself.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art showing a long golden palace facade, hundreds of arched windows glinting in the afternoon sunlight above formal French gardens

facts.quizQuestion: How many emperors of China lived in the Forbidden City across the Ming and Qing dynasties?
facts.explanation: A total of 24 emperors — 14 from the Ming dynasty and 10 from the Qing dynasty — lived in the Forbidden City over more than 500 years. The last emperor, Puyi, was expelled from the palace in 1924, and it became the Palace Museum in 1925.
facts.statement: 24 emperors from the Ming and Qing dynasties lived in the Forbidden City over 500+ years.
facts.wowFactor: The Forbidden City is now the Palace Museum, the world's most visited museum. Its collection contains over 1.8 million artefacts — far more than can ever be displayed at one time.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of 24 imperial portrait plaques arranged in a grid, each showing a robed Chinese emperor against a golden backdrop

facts.quizQuestion: What famous collection of royal regalia is kept and displayed at the Tower of London?
facts.explanation: The British Crown Jewels — including the Imperial State Crown and the Sovereign's Sceptre — are kept in the Jewel House at the Tower of London. The collection includes over 100 objects and dates back centuries. The Tower has served as a royal residence, armoury, treasury, mint, and prison since its founding in 1066.
facts.statement: The Crown Jewels are kept and displayed at the Tower of London.
facts.wowFactor: Anne Boleyn, the second wife of Henry VIII, was executed on Tower Green inside the Tower of London in 1536. Her ghost is said to walk the grounds — making the Tower one of the most famously haunted places in England.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a glittering crown and sceptre displayed behind thick glass in a vaulted stone chamber guarded by a Yeoman Warder

facts.quizQuestion: Which Dalai Lama ordered construction of the modern Potala Palace in Lhasa in 1649?
facts.explanation: The modern Potala Palace was built beginning in 1649 on the orders of the 5th Dalai Lama (Ngawang Lobsang Gyatso). It replaced an earlier structure and became the winter residence of Dalai Lamas. The 14th Dalai Lama used it as his winter palace until fleeing to India in 1959; it is now a museum and UNESCO World Heritage Site.
facts.statement: The 5th Dalai Lama ordered the construction of the modern Potala Palace in 1649.
facts.wowFactor: The Potala Palace rises 119 metres above Marpo Ri hill and more than 300 metres above the valley floor. At 3,700 metres altitude, it is one of the highest palaces in the world — visitors often experience altitude sickness while touring it.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a robed Buddhist monk looking out from a high balcony of a great white-and-red palace, mountains rising behind

facts.quizQuestion: Which flag flies above Buckingham Palace to signal that the monarch is present in the building?
facts.explanation: The Royal Standard flies above Buckingham Palace whenever the reigning monarch is present. When the monarch is away, the Union Flag (commonly called the Union Jack) is flown instead. This tradition dates back centuries and allows the public to know at a glance whether the sovereign is at home.
facts.statement: The Royal Standard flies over Buckingham Palace when the monarch is present; the Union Flag flies when they are absent.
facts.wowFactor: Buckingham Palace was originally built in 1703 as Buckingham House for the Duke of Buckingham. It was not a royal residence at all until Queen Victoria moved in upon her accession in 1837 — before that, the monarchs lived at St James's Palace.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Buckingham Palace's gate and balcony with a bold heraldic flag flying high above a cheering crowd below

facts.quizQuestion: Washington Irving's celebrated book 'Tales of the Alhambra', written after he lived in the palace, was published in what year?
facts.explanation: Washington Irving published 'Tales of the Alhambra' in 1832 after residing in the palace's then-empty apartments. His romantic accounts of Moorish legends and the faded grandeur of the palace sparked enormous international interest in the Alhambra and contributed to efforts to restore and preserve it.
facts.statement: Washington Irving published 'Tales of the Alhambra' in 1832 after living in the palace, helping spark international interest in its preservation.
facts.wowFactor: When Irving stayed in the Alhambra, parts of the palace were in serious disrepair and squatters lived in some of the rooms. His book transformed public perception — the Alhambra became one of the 19th century's most visited sites as a result.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a man writing by candlelight in a Moorish arched room, a quill in hand, moonlight streaming through latticed windows

facts.quizQuestion: Which daimyo commissioned and supervised the construction of Himeji Castle's main keep between 1601 and 1609?
facts.explanation: Ikeda Terumasa, a powerful daimyo and son-in-law of Tokugawa Ieyasu, received the Harima domain as a reward after the Battle of Sekigahara in 1600 and immediately began expanding the castle at Himeji. The result — completed in 1609 — is considered the finest surviving example of Japanese castle architecture.
facts.statement: Ikeda Terumasa built Himeji Castle's main keep between 1601 and 1609.
facts.wowFactor: Himeji Castle survived intact through World War II even though the surrounding city was heavily bombed. One bomb that landed on the castle's top floor was a dud and never exploded — the castle's survival is considered extraordinary good fortune.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a samurai lord in formal court dress presenting a rolled architectural plan to a group of builders before a rising white castle

facts.quizQuestion: King Ludwig II of Bavaria lived in Neuschwanstein Castle for how many days in total before his mysterious death in 1886?
facts.explanation: Despite commissioning Neuschwanstein Castle as his personal retreat in 1869, Ludwig II only lived there for 172 days before his death in 1886. The castle was still unfinished when he died. He was declared mentally unfit and deposed just days before his death — he was found dead in a shallow lake the next day under unexplained circumstances.
facts.statement: Ludwig II lived in Neuschwanstein for only 172 days before his death in 1886 — the castle was never finished.
facts.wowFactor: Neuschwanstein was opened to tourists just seven weeks after Ludwig II's death — his private rooms were immediately turned into a public museum. The castle has now received over 61 million visitors total.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a lone king in royal robes standing at a turret window of an unfinished fairytale castle, looking pensively into misty mountains

facts.quizQuestion: Windsor Castle covers approximately how many acres, making it the largest occupied castle in the world?
facts.explanation: Windsor Castle covers approximately 13 acres (5.26 hectares), making it the largest occupied castle in the world. It has been the home of English and British monarchs for over 900 years. The current monarch uses Windsor Castle as a weekend retreat and for various state occasions.
facts.statement: Windsor Castle covers 13 acres and is the largest occupied castle in the world, used by British monarchs for over 900 years.
facts.wowFactor: St George's Chapel at Windsor Castle is the burial site of ten British monarchs, including Henry VIII. Every year the Castle hosts the Order of the Garter service — England's oldest and most senior order of chivalry, founded by Edward III in 1348.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an aerial view showing a sprawling grey stone castle complex with circular towers set on a grassy hill above a riverside town

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Topkapi Palace in Istanbul converted into a museum, one year after the founding of the Turkish Republic?
facts.explanation: Topkapi Palace became a public museum in 1924, the year after Mustafa Kemal Atatürk founded the Republic of Turkey. The Ottoman sultans had already moved their primary residence to Dolmabahce Palace in 1853. The museum today holds one of the world's great collections of Ottoman art, Chinese porcelain, and Islamic relics.
facts.statement: Topkapi Palace became a museum in 1924, one year after the founding of the Republic of Turkey.
facts.wowFactor: The Topkapi harem — where the sultan's family and concubines lived — had over 400 rooms. At its peak it housed around 1,500 people. The head of the harem, known as the Valide Sultan (queen mother), was often the most powerful woman in the Ottoman Empire.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an ornate domed Ottoman palace gate hung with a museum sign, tourists streaming through beneath a Turkish flag

facts.quizQuestion: Who sculpted the Statue of Liberty, dedicated in New York Harbor in 1886?
facts.explanation: The Statue of Liberty was sculpted by French artist Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi. Gustave Eiffel (of Eiffel Tower fame) engineered the statue's iron internal framework, while architect Richard Morris Hunt designed the pedestal. The statue was a gift from France to the United States, officially named 'Liberty Enlightening the World'.
facts.statement: The Statue of Liberty was sculpted by Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi, with the internal iron framework engineered by Gustave Eiffel.
facts.wowFactor: The statue's copper skin has oxidized over time to its distinctive green colour (verdigris). When it was dedicated in 1886, the copper was a shiny reddish-brown — similar to a new penny. The green patina developed fully by about 1906.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Statue of Liberty in her original copper-brown colour beside a modern version in verdigris green, a boat in the harbour below

facts.quizQuestion: Including its pedestal, what is the total height of the Statue of Liberty from ground to torch?
facts.explanation: The Statue of Liberty stands 46.1 metres (151 feet) tall on its own, but including the pedestal the total height from ground to torch tip is 93.1 metres (305 feet). The statue was dedicated on October 28, 1886, and was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1984.
facts.statement: The Statue of Liberty is 46.1 metres tall; the total height including the pedestal is 93.1 metres.
facts.wowFactor: The statue's official name is 'Liberty Enlightening the World' (La Liberté éclairant le monde). The tablet she holds is inscribed with the Roman numerals for July 4, 1776 — the date of American independence.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a scale comparison showing the Statue of Liberty next to a skyscraper, with a measurement ruler from the ground to the torch tip

facts.quizQuestion: Christ the Redeemer statue in Rio de Janeiro is built in which architectural and artistic style?
facts.explanation: Christ the Redeemer is built in the Art Deco style, constructed of reinforced concrete and covered in soapstone tiles. It was sculpted by French-Polish artist Paul Landowski and engineered by Brazilian Heitor da Silva Costa. The statue was completed in 1931 and stands on Corcovado mountain at 710 metres above Rio de Janeiro.
facts.statement: Christ the Redeemer is an Art Deco statue built of reinforced concrete and soapstone, completed in 1931.
facts.wowFactor: Christ the Redeemer is struck by lightning 3 to 5 times per year. In 2008 and 2014, lightning strikes damaged the statue's fingers and head. The soapstone tiles are replaced regularly as a result.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the outstretched arms of a white stone Art Deco Christ statue, lightning bolts crackling in a stormy sky behind it

facts.quizQuestion: What is the total height of Christ the Redeemer including its pedestal?
facts.explanation: Christ the Redeemer stands 30 metres tall with an additional 8-metre pedestal, giving a total height of 38 metres. It sits atop Corcovado mountain at 710 metres above sea level, making it visible from much of Rio de Janeiro. It was voted one of the New Seven Wonders of the World in 2007.
facts.statement: Christ the Redeemer is 30m tall on an 8m pedestal, totalling 38m, atop Corcovado at 710m altitude.
facts.wowFactor: Corcovado means 'hunchback' in Portuguese, referring to the mountain's distinctive humped silhouette. Despite being repeatedly struck by lightning, the statue has survived intact since 1931 — over 90 years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Christ the Redeemer lit in golden light against a sunset sky, the city of Rio and the bay glittering far below

facts.quizQuestion: Which sculptor directed the carving of Mount Rushmore in South Dakota, beginning in 1927?
facts.explanation: Gutzon Borglum directed the carving of Mount Rushmore from 1927 until his death in 1941, months before the project was completed. His son Lincoln Borglum finished the project. The 60-foot faces of Washington, Jefferson, Theodore Roosevelt, and Lincoln were carved by removing 450,000 tons of rock, primarily using dynamite.
facts.statement: Gutzon Borglum directed the carving of Mount Rushmore from 1927 to 1941, with 450,000 tons of rock removed.
facts.wowFactor: Hidden behind Lincoln's head at Mount Rushmore is a secret Hall of Records — a chamber Borglum planned to store America's most important documents. Only the entrance was carved before funding ran out. The chamber is sealed with a titanium vault containing porcelain enamel panels with the story of the monument.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of four massive presidential faces emerging from grey granite cliffs, pine trees stretching across the Black Hills below

facts.quizQuestion: Which four US presidents are depicted at Mount Rushmore?
facts.explanation: Mount Rushmore depicts George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Theodore Roosevelt, and Abraham Lincoln, chosen by sculptor Gutzon Borglum to represent America's founding, expansion, conservation, and preservation of the Union respectively. The faces are each approximately 60 feet (18 metres) tall.
facts.statement: Mount Rushmore depicts Washington, Jefferson, Theodore Roosevelt, and Lincoln.
facts.wowFactor: Carving at Mount Rushmore employed around 400 workers and not a single person died during construction — an extraordinary safety record for 14 years of mountainside dynamite blasting. Workers wore hardened cloth hats rather than modern hard hats.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of four grey stone presidential faces labelled with names, carved into a cliff face under an American flag

facts.quizQuestion: How many Doric columns does the Lincoln Memorial have, and what do they represent?
facts.explanation: The Lincoln Memorial has 36 Doric columns, one for each state in the Union at the time of Lincoln's death in 1865. The memorial was designed by architect Henry Bacon and dedicated in 1922. The statue of Lincoln inside, 5.8 metres tall, was sculpted by Daniel Chester French.
facts.statement: The Lincoln Memorial has 36 Doric columns — one for each state at the time of Lincoln's death.
facts.wowFactor: Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his 'I Have a Dream' speech from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial on August 28, 1963, before an estimated 250,000 people gathered on the National Mall.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a white neoclassical temple with 36 tall columns, a massive seated stone president visible through the entrance

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Martin Luther King Jr. deliver his 'I Have a Dream' speech from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial?
facts.explanation: Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his famous 'I Have a Dream' speech on August 28, 1963, from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial during the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom. An estimated 250,000 people attended the rally on the National Mall, making it one of the largest political demonstrations in American history.
facts.statement: MLK delivered his 'I Have a Dream' speech at the Lincoln Memorial in 1963 before around 250,000 people.
facts.wowFactor: The exact spot where King stood on the Lincoln Memorial steps is now marked with a circular inscription in the granite reading 'I Have a Dream' with the date August 28, 1963 — added in 2003 on the 40th anniversary.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a man at a podium on white marble steps addressing a vast crowd stretching down the National Mall toward the Washington Monument

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is the Arc de Triomphe in Paris?
facts.explanation: The Arc de Triomphe stands 49.54 metres (162.5 feet) tall. Commissioned by Napoleon Bonaparte in 1806 following his victory at Austerlitz, it was completed in 1836 — after Napoleon's death. Below it lies the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, with an eternal flame that has burned since 1923.
facts.statement: The Arc de Triomphe is 49.54 metres tall, commissioned by Napoleon in 1806 and completed in 1836.
facts.wowFactor: Napoleon never saw his arch completed. For his 1810 wedding procession, a full-scale canvas and timber mock-up of the finished arch was erected on the site so he could parade beneath it. The real thing was still 26 years away.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive stone triumphal arch at the centre of a busy roundabout at night, an eternal flame glowing at its base

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Napoleon Bonaparte commission the Arc de Triomphe in Paris?
facts.explanation: Napoleon commissioned the Arc de Triomphe in 1806 after his decisive victory at the Battle of Austerlitz the previous year. Architect Jean-François Chalgrin designed it. Napoleon died in 1821 and the arch was not completed until 1836 under King Louis-Philippe. Napoleon's ashes passed under the arch in 1840 during their transfer to Les Invalides.
facts.statement: Napoleon commissioned the Arc de Triomphe in 1806 but died in 1821 — 15 years before it was completed.
facts.wowFactor: 12 avenues radiate out from the Arc de Triomphe like spokes of a wheel. The surrounding roundabout is one of the busiest in the world — and has its own rules: once in the roundabout, you have right of way, making it technically every driver for themselves.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Napoleon on horseback saluting a canvas-and-timber mockup arch while the real stone foundations are being laid in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many moai statues were carved by the Rapa Nui people on Easter Island?
facts.explanation: More than 900 moai statues were carved by the Rapa Nui people between approximately 1250 and 1500 CE. They were carved from volcanic tuff quarried at Rano Raraku and represent deified ancestors. The tallest erected moai, known as Paro, stands about 10 metres tall and weighs 82 tonnes. Easter Island is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1995).
facts.statement: The Rapa Nui people carved over 900 moai statues on Easter Island between about 1250 and 1500 CE.
facts.wowFactor: In 2012, researchers demonstrated that the moai could be 'walked' into position upright using ropes and a rocking motion — matching Rapa Nui oral tradition that the statues 'walked' to their platforms on their own.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a row of giant stone heads with heavy brows and long ears standing on a grassy hillside above the Pacific Ocean

facts.quizQuestion: What material were the moai statues of Easter Island primarily carved from?
facts.explanation: The moai were carved primarily from volcanic tuff — a type of compressed volcanic ash — quarried at the Rano Raraku volcanic crater on Easter Island. Roughly 400 unfinished moai remain at the quarry, frozen in various stages of completion. A smaller number of moai were carved from basalt, coral, or red scoria.
facts.statement: The moai of Easter Island were carved from volcanic tuff quarried at the Rano Raraku crater.
facts.wowFactor: The moai only show heads above ground, but many statues have bodies buried beneath the soil. Excavations revealed that some have backs covered in carved petroglyphs — symbols whose meaning is still debated.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of stone carvers at work inside a volcanic crater, chipping away at a half-formed giant face in the grey tuff rock

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is the Gateway of India in Mumbai?
facts.explanation: The Gateway of India stands 26 metres (85 feet) tall and was built to commemorate the visit of King George V and Queen Mary to India in 1911. Designed by architect George Wittet in the Indo-Saracenic style, it was constructed between 1913 and 1924 in Mumbai Harbour.
facts.statement: The Gateway of India in Mumbai is 26 metres tall, built to commemorate King George V's 1911 visit.
facts.wowFactor: In 1948, the last British troops to leave India marched through the Gateway of India and boarded ships — ending 200 years of British rule in a ceremony at the very monument built to celebrate a British king's arrival. The symbolism was deliberate.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a tall yellow basalt archway on a harbour waterfront, small boats in the bay and the Taj Mahal Hotel visible behind it

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the last British troops march through the Gateway of India while departing the country?
facts.explanation: The last British troops to leave India marched through the Gateway of India in 1948, after Indian independence in 1947. The Somerset Light Infantry was the last regiment to leave, boarding ships in Mumbai Harbour in February 1948. The event was deeply symbolic — the monument built to celebrate British imperial arrival had become the exit point for the retreating empire.
facts.statement: The last British troops marched out through the Gateway of India in 1948, ending British rule in India.
facts.wowFactor: The Gateway of India was built in Indo-Saracenic style — a fusion of Islamic, Hindu, and colonial European architecture that the British used to signal respect for Indian traditions while also asserting imperial authority. It is now one of Mumbai's most photographed landmarks.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of uniformed soldiers in colonial dress marching through a monumental arch toward waiting ships on a sun-lit harbour

facts.quizQuestion: Which architect designed the Brandenburg Gate in Berlin, completed in 1791?
facts.explanation: The Brandenburg Gate was designed by Carl Gotthard Langhans and built between 1788 and 1791. It is 26 metres tall and 65.5 metres wide, modelled after the Propylaea gateway to the Acropolis in Athens. It is the only surviving city gate of the original 14 gates of Berlin.
facts.statement: The Brandenburg Gate was designed by Carl Gotthard Langhans and completed in 1791, the only surviving Berlin city gate.
facts.wowFactor: Napoleon seized the bronze quadriga (four-horse chariot) atop the Brandenburg Gate in 1806 and shipped it to Paris as a war trophy. After Napoleon's defeat, the quadriga was returned to Berlin in 1814 — and a Prussian Iron Cross and eagle were added to the wreath the goddess holds, as a victory symbol.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a neoclassical stone gate with six Doric columns and a bronze four-horse chariot on top, the Berlin skyline visible beyond

facts.quizQuestion: The Brandenburg Gate became a symbol of what major historical event in 1989?
facts.explanation: The Brandenburg Gate became a powerful symbol of German reunification in 1989 when the Berlin Wall fell. For 28 years (1961-1989) the gate had stood in the closed border zone between East and West Berlin, inaccessible to citizens of either side. When the Wall opened on November 9, 1989, crowds gathered at the gate in one of the 20th century's most celebrated moments.
facts.statement: The Brandenburg Gate became the symbol of German reunification when the Berlin Wall fell in 1989.
facts.wowFactor: From 1961 to 1989 the Brandenburg Gate stood in the forbidden 'death strip' between East and West Berlin — completely closed to the public from both sides. It stood isolated and silent for 28 years, more a symbol of division than of triumph.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of jubilant crowds climbing atop a broken concrete wall before the Brandenburg Gate, lit up in celebration at night

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is the Washington Monument — the world's tallest obelisk — in Washington DC?
facts.explanation: The Washington Monument stands 169.046 metres (554 feet 7 inches) tall, making it the world's tallest stone obelisk and tallest monumental column. Construction began in 1848 and was completed in 1884 after a 25-year pause. From 1884 to 1889 it was the tallest structure in the world.
facts.statement: The Washington Monument is 169 metres tall, the world's tallest obelisk, completed in 1884.
facts.wowFactor: The Washington Monument has a visible two-tone colour change about a third of the way up — because construction was halted from 1854 to 1879 due to lack of funds and the Civil War. The marble blocks quarried after the pause came from a slightly different part of the quarry and don't quite match.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a towering white stone obelisk reflected in a long rectangular pool, the US Capitol visible in the distant background

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did construction of the Washington Monument halt — creating the visible two-tone colour difference on the obelisk today?
facts.explanation: Construction of the Washington Monument halted in 1854 due to lack of funds and controversy over management of the project, and resumed only in 1879. The 25-year gap caused the visible two-tone band on the obelisk — marble blocks quarried after the pause came from a different section of the quarry and are slightly different in colour.
facts.statement: Construction of the Washington Monument halted in 1854 and resumed in 1879, creating the visible two-tone colour band.
facts.wowFactor: When the Washington Monument was completed in 1884 at 169 metres, it became the tallest structure in the world — surpassing the Cologne Cathedral. It held that record until the Eiffel Tower was completed in 1889.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Washington Monument showing a subtle colour change partway up the obelisk, with a close-up inset showing the band clearly

facts.quizQuestion: Which Mughal emperor built the Taj Mahal in Agra, India, as a mausoleum for his wife?
facts.explanation: The Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal between 1631 and 1653 as a mausoleum for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died in childbirth in 1631. The complex covers 17 hectares and employed approximately 20,000 artisans. It was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1983.
facts.statement: Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal (1631-1653) as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz Mahal.
facts.wowFactor: The four minarets of the Taj Mahal lean very slightly outward — a deliberate engineering decision so that if they collapsed in an earthquake, they would fall away from the central tomb rather than onto it.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Taj Mahal's white marble dome reflected perfectly in a long rectangular pool at sunrise, framed by cypress trees

facts.quizQuestion: What primary material gives the Taj Mahal its brilliant white appearance?
facts.explanation: The Taj Mahal is built primarily from white Makrana marble sourced from Rajasthan. The marble is semi-translucent and changes colour subtly depending on the time of day and light conditions — appearing pinkish at dawn, dazzling white at noon, and golden under moonlight. The main dome is 73 metres tall.
facts.statement: The Taj Mahal is built from white Makrana marble, which changes apparent colour from pink at dawn to gold at night.
facts.wowFactor: Approximately 20,000 artisans worked on the Taj Mahal, including craftsmen from Persia, the Ottoman Empire, and across India. It took 22 years to complete. The inlaid precious and semi-precious stones include jasper, jade, crystal, turquoise, lapis lazuli, and sapphire.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a close-up of the Taj Mahal's white marble wall showing delicate inlaid flower patterns in coloured gemstones

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many artisans worked to build the Taj Mahal in Agra?
facts.explanation: Approximately 20,000 artisans were employed to build the Taj Mahal between 1631 and 1653. They came from across the Mughal Empire and beyond — including craftsmen from Persia, the Ottoman Empire, and Central Asia. The principal architect was Ustad Ahmad Lahori.
facts.statement: Approximately 20,000 artisans built the Taj Mahal over 22 years.
facts.wowFactor: Shah Jahan was later deposed by his own son Aurangzeb and imprisoned in Agra Fort — where, according to tradition, he could see the Taj Mahal from his window. He was buried beside his wife inside the Taj Mahal after his death in 1666.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of hundreds of artisans in turbans working on the Taj Mahal's scaffolding — some carving marble, others inlaying gemstones

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is Paro, the tallest erected moai statue on Easter Island, which weighs approximately 82 tonnes?
facts.explanation: Paro, the tallest erected moai on Easter Island, stands approximately 10 metres (33 feet) tall and weighs approximately 82 tonnes. It stands on the Ahu Te Pito Kura platform on the north coast. Most moai are between 3.5 and 5.5 metres tall; Paro is exceptional in both height and weight.
facts.statement: Paro, the tallest erected moai on Easter Island, stands about 10 metres tall and weighs 82 tonnes.
facts.wowFactor: Many moai have buried torsos with carved patterns on their backs — the famous 'heads' are in fact full-body statues whose lower portions were buried over centuries by soil accumulation. Excavations have revealed tattoo-like patterns carved into the moai's hidden backs.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive stone moai head towering above a person standing beside it for scale, the Pacific Ocean visible in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Who was the French-Polish sculptor who created the figure of Christ the Redeemer in Rio de Janeiro?
facts.explanation: Christ the Redeemer was sculpted by French-Polish artist Paul Landowski, who modelled the figure in clay sections in his Paris studio. Brazilian engineer Heitor da Silva Costa designed the overall structure and supervised construction on Corcovado mountain between 1922 and 1931.
facts.statement: Christ the Redeemer was sculpted by Paul Landowski; Heitor da Silva Costa engineered its construction on Corcovado.
facts.wowFactor: Christ the Redeemer was voted one of the New Seven Wonders of the World in a 2007 global poll. It receives around 2 million visitors per year and is considered a symbol not just of Rio de Janeiro but of Brazil itself.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a sculptor in a Paris studio working on large clay arm sections, with the Corcovado mountain blueprint pinned on the wall behind
